entities.description: Current Oxford Text Archive Cotton SGML-like text file for the shared Owl and Nightingale deposit. It self-identifies Atkins as its source and carries page and folio-column locators, but it is structurally and textually distinct from the repaired MEDictionary/pinned branch.
entities.label: Current OTA U-1684-A Cotton bitstream
notes: The current bitstream has 1,793 tagged L elements plus untagged canonical line 335, duplicates ‘Þe niȝtingale at þisse worde’ where the repaired branch retains an empty node, omits the final canonical line, and contains malformed entity spellings.
Its second folio locator reads 233v.2; the pinned XML, MEDictionary XML, and manuscript sequence read 233r.2. These differences are preserved as version evidence rather than normalized silently.

next_steps: Collate OwlC line by line against Atkins's even-page Cotton transcription and apparatus.
Compare bracketed supplies and asterisk lacunae with both complete manuscript facsimiles.
Do not infer every bracketed character directly from Jesus without checking the corresponding note.

next_steps: Consult the full codicological arguments cited by Fein and the British Library catalog.
Keep X as a supported missing witness layer rather than an extant or authorial source.

reviewNotes: OwlC is the Cotton witness-specific electronic version. OwlJ and the Jesus manuscript are collateral, not upstream physical sources of the Cotton transcription.
Atkins's complete publication contains two parallel transcription streams: Cotton on even pp. 2–150 and Jesus on odd pp. 3–151. OwlC serializes only the Cotton stream.

The current OTA source line says ‘J. W. H. Atkins 1922 edition, NY: Russell and Russell.’ Library of Congress identifies that New York carrier as a 1971 reissue first published in 1922. Carrier dependence is likely but remains qualified until the encoding copy or production documentation is verified.

Current scholarship dates Cotton Caligula A IX to 1250–1299 and leaves the poem's composition date unresolved. The pinned XML's 1190–1210 field and Atkins's earlier manuscript dating are preserved as legacy assertions, not current certainties.

The British Library and Jesus College image licenses are not interchangeable. Jesus images are expressly CC BY 4.0; the British Library IIIF object says public domain in most countries other than the UK, with UK and intended commercial/Git reuse retained as a separate unresolved permissions question.

lineage.summary: The checked-in OwlC XML represents the Cotton Caligula A IX version of The Owl and the Nightingale as transmitted through University of Michigan, Middle English Compendium, and Oxford Text Archive digital layers. The current OTA bitstream bibliographically conflates J. W. H. Atkins's 1922 textual edition with its New York, Russell & Russell 1971 reissue; the 1971 physical carrier is therefore modeled separately as the likely, not proven, encoding carrier. The relevant textual stream is Atkins's Cotton transcription on even printed pages 2–150, based on British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX, ff. 233r–246r. Atkins prints Jesus College MS 29 independently on the facing odd pages; both manuscripts descend independently from a lost exemplar X. Complete IIIF objects for both codices, a complete public scan of the 1922 edition, current digital bitstreams, 1971 carrier metadata and a controlled-borrow scan, and a current Jesus-based scholarly edition are linked with exact work-portion mappings.

catalogSummary.title: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)

catalogingSubject.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous 1,794-line Middle English bird debate as represented by the Cotton Caligula A IX version encoded in U-M CME item OwlC. It is not the complete Cotton codex, the parallel Jesus College MS 29 version encoded as OwlJ, J. W. H. Atkins's complete 1922 edition, or an attempt to reconstruct the lost archetypal work.

contentParts.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)
contentParts.summary: The complete 1,794-position Cotton-version poem cataloged by this record.

assertion.notes: This broad range records the live scholarly dispute, not an evenly probable interval. The conventional 1189–1216 dating and a possible post-1272 date both remain in discussion.

notes: The pinned 1993-era XML header gives 1190–1210. That legacy estimate is preserved as source metadata but is not promoted as a settled modern date.

assertion.notes: This dates the two extant manuscript copies, not composition. Neither is asserted here as uniquely earlier than the other.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the thirteenth century, in Cotton Caligula A IX and Jesus College MS 29

extent.description: One continuous poem conventionally numbered as 1,794 line positions in 897 rhyming couplets. The XML contains 1,795 L nodes: 1,791 hold verbal text, three hold only asterisk lacuna markers at canonical lines 86 and 770–771, and raw position 660 is empty between canonical line 659 (‘Þe niȝtingale at þisse worde’) and canonical line 660 (‘was wel neȝ...’). DIV1 7 and DIV1 17 therefore each contain 101 raw L nodes, and DIV1 18 begins with canonical line 1702 rather than 1701; the supplied final hundred-line boundary is shifted without changing the conventional 1,794-position extent. The eighteen DIV1 groups, seventy-five printed-page milestones, and fifty-two Cotton folio-column milestones are electronic or edition locators rather than intrinsic work divisions; sparse L/@N values are checkpoints, not a consecutive numbering system.

formStatements.description: A continuous poem of 1,794 short lines arranged as 897 rhyming octosyllabic couplets.

assertion.notes: The British Library describes the work as a satirical verse debate, and Fein characterizes its style as lightly satiric; satire is therefore secondary rather than the primary genre.

notes: Cotton Caligula A IX and Jesus College MS 29 are independent copies from lost exemplar X; the Jesus manuscript is collateral evidence for OwlC, not an upstream physical source of the Cotton transcription.

Atkins's older dating of Cotton to the first half of the thirteenth century and the pinned 1190–1210 composition field are historically important but superseded as settled catalog statements by current uncertainty and the current 1250–1299 manuscript catalog date.

The current OTA OwlC SGML is not textually or structurally identical to the pinned XML: it has an untagged canonical line 335, a duplicated line where the repaired branch retains an empty raw node, no final canonical line 1794, a 233v.2/233r.2 locator discrepancy, and malformed entity spellings. The current MEDictionary XML and pinned XML share the repaired 1,795-node/1,794-position stream. The exact transformation history remains unresolved and is documented in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult Cartlidge's full dating argument and subsequent responses rather than inheriting the pinned 1190–1210 estimate.
Keep composition, exemplar, and witness dates separate.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be narrowed responsibly within the competing late-twelfth- through late-thirteenth-century proposals?

next_steps: Retain anonymous primary responsibility unless decisive evidence emerges.
Track Nicholas only as a disputed attributed author and named figure.
openQuestions.question: Was Nicholas of Guildford the poet, the intended arbiter, a patronage addressee, or a fictionalized figure?

next_steps: Review the competing rhyme, dialect, and place-name arguments in current scholarship.
Do not turn Guildford or Portesham references into a certain place of composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the original dialect or place of composition be localized more narrowly than southern England without confusing it with the Cotton copy's West Midlands dialect?

assertion.notes: Southern origin is the broad consensus; the evidence does not securely distinguish Surrey, Dorset, Wessex, the Home Counties, or the south-west Midlands.
The named destination is not by itself proof that the poem was composed in Dorset.

scope.description: Lines 1751–1794 identify Nicholas's home and the birds' destination

assertion.notes: The British Library localizes the Cotton manuscript to the south-west Midlands. This physical-witness statement is separate from the poem's broader and uncertain composition localization.

scope.description: Copying and scribal-language region of the Cotton witness, not a secure place of composition

assertion.notes: No authorship proposal is secure enough to replace anonymous cataloging.
The poem praises Nicholas and proposes him as arbiter, but whether he wrote it cannot be settled. The pinned header correctly calls the attribution former.

scope.description: Cotton text printed on the even-numbered pages 2–150
Facing-page comparison of the Cotton and Jesus versions

assertion.notes: The poem draws on natural science, bestiary traditions, and beast-fable allusions without being reduced to a bestiary entry or beast fable.

scope.description: The closing praise of Nicholas of Guildford, especially lines 1745–1778

assertion.notes: The poem sends the disputants to Nicholas of Guildford but ends before reporting a judgment.

summaries.value: A narrator overhears an owl and a nightingale contest the beauty, morality, and human usefulness of their songs and ways of life. Their wide-ranging exchange treats joy and sorrow, love and marriage, worship, labor, prophecy, reputation, and rhetorical skill. When rival companies of birds threaten violence, a wren restores the king's peace, and the disputants set out for Portesham to seek judgment from Nicholas of Guildford; the poem ends without reporting a verdict.

OwlC serializes only Atkins's Cotton text, printed on even pages 2–150. The Jesus text on the facing odd pages is a parallel witness with its own electronic item, OwlJ. The eighteen numbered DIV1 elements are supplied hundred-line navigation groups, not medieval sections.

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this record from the parallel Jesus version; it is not asserted as a medieval title.

Their wide-ranging exchange treats joy and sorrow, love and marriage, worship, labor, prophecy, reputation, and rhetorical skill. When rival companies of birds threaten violence, a wren restores the king's peace, and the disputants set out for Portesham to seek judgment from Nicholas of Guildford; the poem ends without reporting a verdict.
